Math4121Exam reviewsMath4121 Final Review

Math4121 Final Review

Guidelines

There is one question from Exam 2 material.

3 T/F from Exam 1 material.

The remaining questions cover the material since Exam 2 (Chapters 5 and 6 of Bressoud and my lecture notes for the final week).

The format of the exam is quite similar to Exam 2, maybe a tad longer (but not twice as long, don’t worry).

Chapter 5: Measure Theory

Jordan Measure

Content

Let CSe\mathcal{C}_S^eCSe​ be the set of all finite covers of SSS by closed intervals (S⊂CS\subset CS⊂C, where CCC is a finite union of closed intervals).

Let CSi\mathcal{C}_S^iCSi​ be the set of disjoint intervals that contained in SSS (⋃i=1nIi⊂S\bigcup_{i=1}^n I_i\subset S⋃i=1n​Ii​⊂S, where IiI_iIi​ are disjoint intervals).

Let ce(S)=sup⁡C∈CSe∑i=1n∣Ii∣c_e(S)=\sup_{C\in\mathcal{C}_S^e} \sum_{i=1}^n |I_i|ce​(S)=supC∈CSe​​∑i=1n​∣Ii​∣ be the outer content of SSS.

Let ci(S)=inf⁡I∈CSi∑i=1n∣Ii∣c_i(S)=\inf_{I\in\mathcal{C}_S^i} \sum_{i=1}^n |I_i|ci​(S)=infI∈CSi​​∑i=1n​∣Ii​∣ be the inner content of SSS.

Here we use ∣I∣|I|∣I∣ to denote the length of the interval III, in book we use volume but that’s not important here.

The content of SSS is defined if c(S)=ce(S)=ci(S)c(S)=c_e(S)=c_i(S)c(S)=ce​(S)=ci​(S)

Note that from this definition, for any pairwise disjoint collection of sets S1,S2,⋯ ,SNS_1, S_2, \cdots, S_NS1​,S2​,⋯,SN​, we have

∑i=1Nci(Si)≤ci(⋃i=1NSi)≤ce(⋃i=1NSi)≤∑i=1Nce(Si)\sum_{i=1}^N c_i(S_i)\leq c_i(\bigcup_{i=1}^N S_i)\leq c_e(\bigcup_{i=1}^N S_i)\leq \sum_{i=1}^N c_e(S_i)i=1∑N​ci​(Si​)≤ci​(i=1⋃N​Si​)≤ce​(i=1⋃N​Si​)≤i=1∑N​ce​(Si​)

by sup⁡\supsup and inf⁡\infinf in the definition of ce(S)c_e(S)ce​(S) and ci(S)c_i(S)ci​(S).

Proposition 5.1

ce(S)=ci(S)+ce(∂S)c_e(S)=c_i(S)+c_e(\partial S)ce​(S)=ci​(S)+ce​(∂S)

Note the boundary of SSS is defined as ∂S=S‾∖S∘\partial S=\overline{S}\setminus S^\circ∂S=S∖S∘ (corrected by Nathan Zhou).

Some common notations for sets:

S∘S^\circS∘ is the interior of SSS. S∘={x∈S∣∃ϵ>0,B(x,ϵ)⊂S}S^\circ=\{x\in S| \exists \epsilon>0, B(x,\epsilon)\subset S\}S∘={x∈S∣∃ϵ>0,B(x,ϵ)⊂S} (largest open set contained in SSS)

S′S'S′ is the set of limit points of SSS (derived set of SSS). S′={x∈Rn∣∀ϵ>0,B(x,ϵ)∖{x}∩S≠∅}S'=\{x\in \mathbb{R}^n|\forall \epsilon>0, B(x,\epsilon)\setminus \{x\}\cap S\neq \emptyset\}S′={x∈Rn∣∀ϵ>0,B(x,ϵ)∖{x}∩S=∅} (Topological definition of limit point).

S‾\overline{S}S is the closure of SSS. S‾=S∪S′\overline{S}=S\cup S'S=S∪S′ (smallest closed set containing SSS)

Equivalently, ∀x∈∂S\forall x\in \partial S∀x∈∂S, ∀ϵ>0\forall \epsilon>0∀ϵ>0, ∃p∉S\exists p\notin S∃p∈/S and q∉Sq\notin Sq∈/S s.t. d(x,p)<ϵd(x,p)<\epsilond(x,p)<ϵ and d(x,q)<ϵd(x,q)<\epsilond(x,q)<ϵ.

So the content of SSS is defined if and only if ce(∂S)=0c_e(\partial S)=0ce​(∂S)=0.

Jordan Measurable

A set SSS is Jordan measurable if and only if ce(∂S)=0c_e(\partial S)=0ce​(∂S)=0, (c(S)=ce(S)=ci(S)c(S)=c_e(S)=c_i(S)c(S)=ce​(S)=ci​(S))

Proposition 5.2

Finite additivity of content:

Let S1,S2,⋯ ,SNS_1, S_2, \cdots, S_NS1​,S2​,⋯,SN​ be a finite collection of pairwise disjoint Jordan measurable sets.

c(⋃i=1NSi)=∑i=1Nc(Si)c(\bigcup_{i=1}^N S_i)=\sum_{i=1}^N c(S_i)c(i=1⋃N​Si​)=i=1∑N​c(Si​)

Example for Jordan measure of sets

Borel Measure

Our desired property of measures:

  1. Measure of interval is the length of the interval. m([a,b])=m((a,b))=m([a,b))=m((a,b])=b−am([a,b])=m((a,b))=m([a,b))=m((a,b])=b-am([a,b])=m((a,b))=m([a,b))=m((a,b])=b−a

  2. Countable additivity: If S1,S2,⋯ ,SNS_1, S_2, \cdots, S_NS1​,S2​,⋯,SN​ are pairwise disjoint Borel measurable sets, then m(⋃i=1NSi)=∑i=1Nm(Si)m(\bigcup_{i=1}^N S_i)=\sum_{i=1}^N m(S_i)m(⋃i=1N​Si​)=∑i=1N​m(Si​)

  3. Closure under set minus: If SSS is Borel measurable and TTT is Borel measurable, then S∖TS\setminus TS∖T is Borel measurable with m(S∖T)=m(S)−m(T)m(S\setminus T)=m(S)-m(T)m(S∖T)=m(S)−m(T)

Borel Measurable Sets

B\mathcal{B}B is the smallest σ\sigmaσ-algebra that contains all closed intervals.

Sigma algebra: A σ\sigmaσ-algebra is a collection of sets that is closed under countable union, intersection, and complement.

That is:

  1. ∅∈B\emptyset\in \mathcal{B}∅∈B
  2. If A∈BA\in \mathcal{B}A∈B, then Ac∈BA^c\in \mathcal{B}Ac∈B
  3. If A1,A2,⋯ ,AN∈BA_1, A_2, \cdots, A_N\in \mathcal{B}A1​,A2​,⋯,AN​∈B, then ⋃i=1NAi∈B\bigcup_{i=1}^N A_i\in \mathcal{B}⋃i=1N​Ai​∈B

Proposition 5.3

Borel measurable sets does not contain all Jordan measurable sets.

Proof by cardinality of sets.

Example for Borel measure of sets

SetBorel Measure
∅\emptyset∅0
{q},q∈R\{q\},q\in \mathbb{R}{q},q∈R0

{[n,n+12n]}n=1∞\{[n,n+\frac{1}{2^n}]\}_{n=1}^\infty{[n,n+2n1​]}n=1∞​1

SVC(4)SVC(4)SVC(4)0
Q∩[0,1]Q\cap [0,1]Q∩[0,1]0
[0,1]∖Q[0,1]\setminus Q[0,1]∖Q1
[a,b],a<b∈R[a,b], a<b\in \mathbb{R}[a,b],a<b∈Rb−ab-ab−a
[a,b),a<b∈R[a,b),a<b\in \mathbb{R}[a,b),a<b∈Rb−ab-ab−a
(a,b],a<b∈R(a,b],a<b\in \mathbb{R}(a,b],a<b∈Rb−ab-ab−a
(a,b),a<b∈R(a,b),a<b\in \mathbb{R}(a,b),a<b∈Rb−ab-ab−a

Lebesgue Measure

Lebesgue measure

Let C\mathcal{C}C be the set of all countable covers of SSS.

The Lebesgue outer measure of SSS is defined as:

me(S)=inf⁡C∈C∑i=1∞∣Ii∣m_e(S)=\inf_{C\in\mathcal{C}} \sum_{i=1}^\infty |I_i|me​(S)=infC∈C​∑i=1∞​∣Ii​∣

If S⊂[a,b]S\subset[a,b]S⊂[a,b], then the inner measure of SSS is defined as:

mi(S)=(b−a)−me([a,b]∖S)m_i(S)=(b-a)-m_e([a,b]\setminus S)mi​(S)=(b−a)−me​([a,b]∖S)

If mi(S)=me(S)m_i(S)=m_e(S)mi​(S)=me​(S), then SSS is Lebesgue measurable.

Proposition 5.4

Subadditivity of Lebesgue outer measure:

For any collection of sets S1,S2,⋯ ,SNS_1, S_2, \cdots, S_NS1​,S2​,⋯,SN​,

me(⋃i=1NSi)≤∑i=1Nme(Si)m_e(\bigcup_{i=1}^N S_i)\leq \sum_{i=1}^N m_e(S_i)me​(⋃i=1N​Si​)≤∑i=1N​me​(Si​)

Theorem 5.5

If SSS is bounded, then any of the following conditions imply that SSS is Lebesgue measurable:

  1. me(S)=0m_e(S)=0me​(S)=0
  2. SSS is countable (measure of countable set is 0)
  3. SSS is an interval

Alternative definition of Lebesgue measure

The outer measure of SSS is defined as the infimum of all the open sets that contain SSS.

The inner measure of SSS is defined as the supremum of all the closed sets that are contained in SSS.

Theorem 5.6

Caratheodory’s criterion:

A set SSS is Lebesgue measurable if and only if for any set XXX with finite outer measure,

me(X−S)=me(X)−me(X∩S)m_e(X-S)=m_e(X)-m_e(X\cap S)me​(X−S)=me​(X)−me​(X∩S)

Lemma 5.7

Local additivity of Lebesgue outer measure:

If I1,I2,⋯ ,INI_1, I_2, \cdots, I_NI1​,I2​,⋯,IN​ are any countable collection of pairwise disjoint intervals and SSS is a bounded set, then

me(S∪⋃i=1NIi)=∑i=1Nme(S∩Ii)m_e\left(S\cup \bigcup_{i=1}^N I_i\right)=\sum_{i=1}^N m_e(S\cap I_i)me​(S∪i=1⋃N​Ii​)=i=1∑N​me​(S∩Ii​)

Theorem 5.8

Countable additivity of Lebesgue outer measure:

If S1,S2,⋯ ,SNS_1, S_2, \cdots, S_NS1​,S2​,⋯,SN​ are any countable collection of pairwise disjoint Lebesgue measurable sets, whose union has a finite outer measure, then

me(⋃i=1NSi)=∑i=1Nme(Si)m_e\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^N S_i\right)=\sum_{i=1}^N m_e(S_i)me​(i=1⋃N​Si​)=i=1∑N​me​(Si​)

Theorem 5.9

Any finite union or intersection of Lebesgue measurable sets is Lebesgue measurable.

Theorem 5.10

Any countable union or intersection of Lebesgue measurable sets is Lebesgue measurable.

Corollary 5.12

Limit of a monotone sequence of Lebesgue measurable sets is Lebesgue measurable.

If S1⊆S2⊆S3⊆⋯S_1\subseteq S_2\subseteq S_3\subseteq \cdotsS1​⊆S2​⊆S3​⊆⋯ are Lebesgue measurable sets, then ⋃i=1∞Si\bigcup_{i=1}^\infty S_i⋃i=1∞​Si​ is Lebesgue measurable. And m(⋃i=1∞Si)=lim⁡i→∞m(Si)m(\bigcup_{i=1}^\infty S_i)=\lim_{i\to\infty} m(S_i)m(⋃i=1∞​Si​)=limi→∞​m(Si​)

If S1⊇S2⊇S3⊇⋯S_1\supseteq S_2\supseteq S_3\supseteq \cdotsS1​⊇S2​⊇S3​⊇⋯ are Lebesgue measurable sets, and S1S_1S1​ has finite measure, then ⋂i=1∞Si\bigcap_{i=1}^\infty S_i⋂i=1∞​Si​ is Lebesgue measurable. And m(⋂i=1∞Si)=lim⁡i→∞m(Si)m(\bigcap_{i=1}^\infty S_i)=\lim_{i\to\infty} m(S_i)m(⋂i=1∞​Si​)=limi→∞​m(Si​)

Theorem 5.13

Non-measurable sets (under axiom of choice)

Note that (0,1)⊆⋃q∈Q∩(−1,1)(N+q)⊆(−1,2)(0,1)\subseteq \bigcup_{q\in \mathbb{Q}\cap (-1,1)}(\mathcal{N}+q)\subseteq (-1,2)(0,1)⊆⋃q∈Q∩(−1,1)​(N+q)⊆(−1,2)

⋃q∈Q∩(−1,1)(N+q)\bigcup_{q\in \mathbb{Q}\cap (-1,1)}(\mathcal{N}+q)q∈Q∩(−1,1)⋃​(N+q)

is not Lebesgue measurable.

Chapter 6: Lebesgue Integration

Lebesgue Integral

Let the partition on y-axis be l=l0<l1<⋯<ln=Ll=l_0<l_1<\cdots<l_n=Ll=l0​<l1​<⋯<ln​=L, and Si={x∣li<f(x)<li+1}S_i=\{x|l_i<f(x)<l_{i+1}\}Si​={x∣li​<f(x)<li+1​}

The Lebesgue integral of fff over [a,b][a,b][a,b] is bounded by:

∑i=0n−1lim(Si)≤∫abf(x) dx≤∑i=0n−1li+1m(Si)\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} l_i m(S_i)\leq \int_a^b f(x) \, dx\leq \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} l_{i+1} m(S_i)i=0∑n−1​li​m(Si​)≤∫ab​f(x)dx≤i=0∑n−1​li+1​m(Si​)

Definition of measurable function:

A function fff is measurable if for all c∈Rc\in \mathbb{R}c∈R, the set {x∈[a,b]∣f(x)>c}\{x\in [a,b]|f(x)>c\}{x∈[a,b]∣f(x)>c} is Lebesgue measurable.

Equivalently, a function fff is measurable if any of the following conditions hold:

  1. For all c∈Rc\in \mathbb{R}c∈R, the set {x∈[a,b]∣f(x)>c}\{x\in [a,b]|f(x)>c\}{x∈[a,b]∣f(x)>c} is Lebesgue measurable.
  2. For all c∈Rc\in \mathbb{R}c∈R, the set {x∈[a,b]∣f(x)≥c}\{x\in [a,b]|f(x)\geq c\}{x∈[a,b]∣f(x)≥c} is Lebesgue measurable.
  3. For all c∈Rc\in \mathbb{R}c∈R, the set {x∈[a,b]∣f(x)<c}\{x\in [a,b]|f(x)<c\}{x∈[a,b]∣f(x)<c} is Lebesgue measurable.
  4. For all c∈Rc\in \mathbb{R}c∈R, the set {x∈[a,b]∣f(x)≤c}\{x\in [a,b]|f(x)\leq c\}{x∈[a,b]∣f(x)≤c} is Lebesgue measurable.
  5. For all c<d∈Rc<d\in \mathbb{R}c<d∈R, the set {x∈[a,b]∣c≤f(x)<d}\{x\in [a,b]|c\leq f(x)<d\}{x∈[a,b]∣c≤f(x)<d} is Lebesgue measurable.

Prove by using the fact{x∈[a,b]∣f(x)≥c}=⋂n=1∞{x∈[a,b]∣f(x)>c−1n}\{x\in [a,b]|f(x)\geq c\}=\bigcap_{n=1}^\infty \{x\in [a,b]|f(x)>c-\frac{1}{n}\}{x∈[a,b]∣f(x)≥c}=⋂n=1∞​{x∈[a,b]∣f(x)>c−n1​}

Proposition 6.3

If f,gf,gf,g is a measurable function, and k∈Rk\in \mathbb{R}k∈R, then f+g,kf,f2,fg,∣f∣f+g,kf,f^2,fg,|f|f+g,kf,f2,fg,∣f∣ is measurable.

Definition of almost everywhere:

A property holds almost everywhere if it holds everywhere except for a set of Lebesgue measure 0.

Proposition 6.4

If fnf_nfn​ is a sequence of measurable functions, then lim sup⁡n→∞fn,lim inf⁡n→∞fn\limsup_{n\to\infty} f_n, \liminf_{n\to\infty} f_nlimsupn→∞​fn​,liminfn→∞​fn​ is measurable.

Theorem 6.5

Limit of measurable functions is measurable.

Definition of simple function:

A simple function is a linear combination of indicator functions of Lebesgue measurable sets.

Theorem 6.6

Measurable function as limit of simple functions.

fff is a measurable function if and only if ffthere exists a sequence of simple functions fnf_nfn​ s.t. fn→ff_n\to ffn​→f almost everywhere.

Integration

Proposition 6.10

Let ϕ,ψ\phi,\psiϕ,ψ be simple functions, c∈Rc\in \mathbb{R}c∈R and E=E1∪E2E=E_1\cup E_2E=E1​∪E2​ where E1∩E2=∅E_1\cap E_2=\emptysetE1​∩E2​=∅.

Then

  1. ∫Eϕ(x) dx=∫E1ϕ(x) dx+∫E2ϕ(x) dx\int_E \phi(x) \, dx=\int_{E_1} \phi(x) \, dx+\int_{E_2} \phi(x) \, dx∫E​ϕ(x)dx=∫E1​​ϕ(x)dx+∫E2​​ϕ(x)dx
  2. ∫E(cϕ)(x) dx=c∫Eϕ(x) dx\int_E (c\phi)(x) \, dx=c\int_E \phi(x) \, dx∫E​(cϕ)(x)dx=c∫E​ϕ(x)dx
  3. ∫E(ϕ+ψ)(x) dx=∫Eϕ(x) dx+∫Eψ(x) dx\int_E (\phi+\psi)(x) \, dx=\int_E \phi(x) \, dx+\int_E \psi(x) \, dx∫E​(ϕ+ψ)(x)dx=∫E​ϕ(x)dx+∫E​ψ(x)dx
  4. If ϕ≤ψ\phi\leq \psiϕ≤ψ for all x∈Ex\in Ex∈E, then ∫Eϕ(x) dx≤∫Eψ(x) dx\int_E \phi(x) \, dx\leq \int_E \psi(x) \, dx∫E​ϕ(x)dx≤∫E​ψ(x)dx

Definition of Lebesgue integral of simple function:

Let ϕ\phiϕ be a simple function, ϕ=∑i=1nliχSi\phi=\sum_{i=1}^n l_i \chi_{S_i}ϕ=∑i=1n​li​χSi​​

∫Eϕ(x) dx=∑i=1nlim(Si∩E)\int_E \phi(x) \, dx=\sum_{i=1}^n l_i m(S_i\cap E)∫E​ϕ(x)dx=∑i=1n​li​m(Si​∩E)

Definition of Lebesgue integral of measurable function:

Let fff be a nonnegative measurable function, then

∫Ef(x) dx=sup⁡ϕ≤f∫Eϕ(x) dx\int_E f(x) \, dx=\sup_{\phi\leq f} \int_E \phi(x) \, dx∫E​f(x)dx=supϕ≤f​∫E​ϕ(x)dx

If fff is not nonnegative, then

∫Ef(x) dx=∫Ef+(x) dx−∫Ef−(x) dx\int_E f(x) \, dx=\int_E f^+(x) \, dx-\int_E f^-(x) \, dx∫E​f(x)dx=∫E​f+(x)dx−∫E​f−(x)dx

where f+(x)=max⁡(f(x),0)f^+(x)=\max(f(x),0)f+(x)=max(f(x),0) and f−(x)=max⁡(−f(x),0)f^-(x)=\max(-f(x),0)f−(x)=max(−f(x),0)

Proposition 6.12

Integral over a set of measure 0 is 0.

Theorem 6.13

If a nonnegative measurable function fff has integral 0 on a set EEE, then f(x)=0f(x)=0f(x)=0 almost everywhere on EEE.

Theorem 6.14

Monotone convergence theorem:

If fnf_nfn​ is a sequence of monotone increasing measurable functions and fn→ff_n\to ffn​→f almost everywhere, and ∃A>0\exists A>0∃A>0 s.t. ∣∫Efn(x) dx∣≤A|\int_E f_n(x) \, dx|\leq A∣∫E​fn​(x)dx∣≤A for all nnn, then f(x)=lim⁡n→∞fn(x)f(x)=\lim_{n\to\infty} f_n(x)f(x)=limn→∞​fn​(x) exists almost everywhere and it’s integrable on EEE with

∫Ef(x) dx=lim⁡n→∞∫Efn(x) dx\int_E f(x) \, dx=\lim_{n\to\infty} \int_E f_n(x) \, dx∫E​f(x)dx=n→∞lim​∫E​fn​(x)dx

Theorem 6.19

Dominated convergence theorem:

If fnf_nfn​ is a sequence of integrable functions and fn→ff_n\to ffn​→f almost everywhere, and there exists a nonnegative integrable function ggg s.t. ∣fn(x)∣≤g(x)|f_n(x)|\leq g(x)∣fn​(x)∣≤g(x) for all x∈Ex\in Ex∈E and all nnn, then f(x)=lim⁡n→∞fn(x)f(x)=\lim_{n\to\infty} f_n(x)f(x)=limn→∞​fn​(x) exists almost everywhere and it’s integrable on EEE with

∫Ef(x) dx=lim⁡n→∞∫Efn(x) dx\int_E f(x) \, dx=\lim_{n\to\infty} \int_E f_n(x) \, dx∫E​f(x)dx=n→∞lim​∫E​fn​(x)dx

Theorem 6.20

Fatou’s lemma:

If fnf_nfn​ is a sequence of nonnegative integrable functions, then

∫Elim inf⁡n→∞fn(x) dx≤lim inf⁡n→∞∫Efn(x) dx\int_E \liminf_{n\to\infty} f_n(x) \, dx\leq \liminf_{n\to\infty} \int_E f_n(x) \, dx∫E​n→∞liminf​fn​(x)dx≤n→∞liminf​∫E​fn​(x)dx

Definition of Hardy-Littlewood maximal function

Given integrable fffm and an interval III, look at the averaging operator AIf(x)=χI(x)m(I)∫If(y)dyA_I f(x)=\frac{\chi_I(x)}{m(I)}\int_I f(y)dyAI​f(x)=m(I)χI​(x)​∫I​f(y)dy.

The maximal function is defined as

f∗(x)=sup⁡I is an open intervalAIf(x)f^*(x)=\sup_{I \text{ is an open interval}} A_I f(x)f∗(x)=supI is an open interval​AI​f(x)

Lebesgue’s Fundamental theorem of calculus

If fff is Lebesgue integrable on [a,b][a,b][a,b], then F(x)=∫axf(t)dtF(x) = \int_a^x f(t)dtF(x)=∫ax​f(t)dt is differentiable almost everywhere and F′(x)=f(x)F'(x) = f(x)F′(x)=f(x) almost everywhere.

Outline:

Let λ,ϵ>0\lambda,\epsilon > 0λ,ϵ>0. Find ggg continuous such that ∫R∣f−g∣dm<λϵ5\int_{\mathbb{R}}|f-g|dm < \frac{\lambda \epsilon}{5}∫R​∣f−g∣dm<5λϵ​.

To control AIf(x)−f(x)=(AI(f−g)(x))+(AIg(x)−g(x))+(g(x)−f(x))A_I f(x)-f(x)=(A_I(f-g)(x))+(A_I g(x)-g(x))+(g(x)-f(x))AI​f(x)−f(x)=(AI​(f−g)(x))+(AI​g(x)−g(x))+(g(x)−f(x)), we need to estimate the three terms separately.

Our goal is to show that lim⁡r→0+sup⁡I is open interval,m(I)<r,x∈I∣AIf(x)−f(x)∣=0\lim_{r\to 0^+}\sup_{I\text{ is open interval}, m(I)<r, x\in I}|A_I f(x)-f(x)|=0limr→0+​supI is open interval,m(I)<r,x∈I​∣AI​f(x)−f(x)∣=0. For xxx almost every x∈[a,b]x\in[a,b]x∈[a,b].
